title: France's deadly June heatwave caused nearly 5,800 excess deaths, highest toll since 2003 - The Times of India
search_term: (heatwave OR "heat wave" OR "severe heatwave" OR "extreme heat" OR "heat spell" OR "scorching heat" OR "blistering heat" OR "sweltering heat" OR "hot winds" OR loo OR "hot loo winds" OR "intense heat" OR "unbearable heat" OR "deadly heat" OR heat OR "weather forecast" OR "weather update" OR "weather alert" OR "IMD forecast" OR "IMD prediction" OR "IMD alert" OR IMD OR "rising temperatures" OR "temperatures expected to rise" OR "relief from heat" OR "respite from heat" OR "heat respite" OR "respite from scorching heat") Andaman and Nicobar Islands
full_text:
France recorded 5,764 more deaths than expected during a record-breaking heatwave that swept the country between June 17 and July 2, health authorities said on Wednesday, highlighting the deadly impact of increasingly frequent extreme weather events linked to climate change.
According to France's public health agency, 21,674 people died from all causes during the period, compared with an expected 15,910 deaths based on historical trends.
The resulting excess mortality of 36 per cent marks the country's highest heatwave-related death toll since the catastrophic summer of 2003, when around 15,000 people died, as per news agency AP.
The latest estimate is significantly higher than the initial figure of about 2,025 excess deaths announced by French health minister Stephanie Rist for late June.
Paris worst-hit as temperatures shattered records
France experienced its hottest-ever days on June 24 and June 25, with the national average temperature reaching 30 degrees Celsius, while many regions saw daytime highs exceeding 40 degrees Celsius.
The country also recorded its hottest June since records began in 1947.
The public health agency said more than half of the excess deaths occurred between June 25 and June 27, when temperatures peaked.
The Paris region suffered the highest toll, recording 1,999 excess deaths—around 80 per cent more than would normally be expected during the period.
Funeral service providers in the capital reportedly struggled to find space to store bodies before burial or cremation, with some mortuaries reaching capacity.
The agency described the episode as "unprecedented", noting that its early onset, intensity and duration exposed nearly the entire population to extreme heat while schools and workplaces were still operating.
Elderly most affected, hospitals overwhelmed
People aged 75 years and above accounted for around two-thirds of the excess deaths.
Nearly half of all those who died during the period passed away in hospitals, which faced a surge in patients suffering from heat-related illnesses.
About one-third died at home, while most of the remaining deaths occurred in care homes for older people.
Authorities stressed that the figures cover all-cause mortality rather than only deaths officially attributed to heat. Experts say many heat-related fatalities are never formally recorded as such because extreme heat often worsens existing conditions such as heart disease, with death certificates listing the immediate medical cause instead.
The health agency said the latest toll remains preliminary and will be updated later this year when more complete mortality data becomes available.
Scientists have increasingly linked Europe's recurring and more intense heatwaves to human-induced climate change.
France has recorded 53 nationwide heatwaves since 1947, with half of them occurring since 2010, underlining the growing frequency of extreme heat events.

title: Red Alert in South Gujarat; Rescue Teams Intensify Evacuation Efforts - DD News
search_term: ("heat advisory" OR "heat action plan" OR "heat alert" OR "IMD warning" OR "IMD alert" OR "red alert" OR "orange alert" OR "yellow alert" OR "NDMA advisory" OR "cooling shelter" OR "relief measures" OR "heat compensation" OR "severe weather warning") Andaman and Nicobar Islands
full_text:
Rescue and relief operations have been intensified across South Gujarat as heavy rainfall continues to affect several districts, with the deployment of National Disaster Response Force (NDRF), State Disaster Response Force (SDRF) and Army personnel in the worst-hit areas.
According to an official release, three NDRF teams and five SDRF teams are currently deployed in Valsad district to assist the local administration in rescue, relief and evacuation operations. Two additional NDRF teams from New Delhi are expected to arrive later on Thursday.
To strengthen the response, a contingent of 70 Army personnel, equipped with five boats, along with a 25-member Fire Department team, is also being deployed in Valsad to support ongoing operations.
Gujarat Chief Minister Bhupendra Patel has deputed senior ministers and officials to supervise relief efforts in the affected districts.
According to the Commissionerate of Information, Deputy Chief Minister Harsh Sanghavi, Tribal Development Minister Naresh Patel and senior secretaries have been assigned to assist district administrations in Surat, Valsad and Navsari, which have been severely affected by heavy rainfall. In-charge secretaries have also been directed to immediately reach districts placed under Red Alert to oversee preparedness and response measures.
South Gujarat remained on high alert on Thursday as intense rainfall led to widespread waterlogging, rising river levels and large-scale evacuations. Authorities have ordered the closure of schools and colleges in Surat, Navsari and Valsad as a precautionary measure.
The India Meteorological Department (IMD) has forecast extremely heavy rainfall across several districts of Gujarat over the next three days, prompting district administrations and the Surat Municipal Corporation to maintain round-the-clock rescue and relief operations.
Surat city continues to remain under a Red Alert. District Collector Tejas Parmar said the administration is fully prepared to respond to the evolving situation and confirmed that evacuation of residents from low-lying areas has already begun with the assistance of NDRF and other relief teams.
He appealed to people living in vulnerable areas to cooperate with authorities during evacuation efforts and urged citizens to rely only on official information while avoiding rumours.
-ANI
